title: The Stone or the Brick—Which Are You?
excerpt: Are you living like a brick—safe, structured, and unmoving—or like a stone, willing to be moved, shaped, and used to open the way for something greater?
content:
Last night at church, something simple stuck with me. You know how that happens sometimes. It’s not always the long explanations or the big moments. Sometimes it’s one image, one comparison, that quietly sits with you and won’t let go.

The focus was on the stone at the tomb of Jesus. That stone wasn’t meant to move. It was placed there to seal something shut. To mark an ending. To say, this is over.

But we all know how that story goes.

That stone didn’t stay put.

And then came a comparison I hadn’t really thought about before. A brick versus a stone.

A brick is structured. Manufactured. Designed to stay exactly where it’s placed. Stack it, line it up, build something with it, and it does its job. It holds firm. It doesn’t shift. It doesn’t adapt. It doesn’t move unless something forces it to.

There’s a certain comfort in that, isn’t there? Predictable. Stable. Safe.

But also… stagnant.

A stone, on the other hand, is different. It’s not perfectly shaped. It’s been through things. Weathered. Worn down. Moved by water, by time, by pressure. And because of that, it changes. It ends up in places it wasn’t originally meant to be. It gets turned over. It exposes what’s underneath. It contributes to something bigger simply by being willing to move.

That got me thinking.

Which one are we?

Because if we’re honest, it’s easy to become a brick. We find our spot in life, we get comfortable, we build our routines, and we stay there. Same mindset. Same patterns. Same way of doing things. We convince ourselves that staying put is the safest way to live.

And maybe it is.

But nothing grows there.

Being a moving stone? That’s different. That takes a willingness to be uncomfortable. To be shifted. To let life, and more importantly God, move you where you need to go, not just where you want to stay. It means being open to change, even when it doesn’t make sense in the moment.

Because here’s the thing about that stone at the tomb. When it moved, it didn’t just change its own position. It revealed something greater. It opened the way for hope, for new life, for something no one expected.

That’s what moving stones do.

They don’t just change themselves. They create space for something bigger.

Maybe that looks like stepping into a new opportunity you’ve been avoiding. Maybe it’s having a conversation you’ve been putting off. Maybe it’s letting go of something that’s kept you stuck for far too long. Or maybe it’s simply choosing to show up differently for the people around you.

It doesn’t have to be dramatic.

But it does have to move.

Bricks build walls. Stones, when they move, can open doors.

So the question is simple.

Are you staying exactly where you’ve always been, holding your place, playing it safe?

Or are you willing to be moved, shaped, and used in a way that might just change not only your life, but someone else’s too?

Because one stays the same.

The other makes a difference.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.

title: Finding JOY—The Order That Changes Everything
excerpt: True joy isn’t something you chase—it’s found when you put Jesus first, serve others well, and trust that you’ll be taken care of along the way.
content:
Think about the word joy for a moment.
 Not the kind that comes and goes with a good day or a lucky break, but the kind that sticks with you even when life doesn’t go according to plan. We all want it. We chase it in different ways, hoping the next achievement, the next moment, or even the next quiet day will finally bring it. But what if joy isn’t something you stumble into? What if it’s something you build—intentionally, steadily, and with purpose?

There’s a simple way to look at it: J-O-Y. Not just a word, but a way of living that, when you get it right, seems to bring everything else back into focus.

J is for Jesus.
Now stay with me here. This isn’t about checking a box on Sunday morning or posting a verse and calling it a day. This is about foundation. When your life is rooted in something bigger than yourself, something steady and unchanging, it shifts how you see everything else. Storms still come. Hard days still show up uninvited. But there’s a peace underneath it all that doesn’t move. That’s where real joy starts. Not in perfection, but in presence. Not in control, but in trust.

O is for Others.
And this is where things start to get real. Because putting others before yourself goes against just about everything the world pushes these days. We’re told to look out for number one, protect our time, guard our energy. And sure, there’s a place for boundaries. But there’s also something deeply fulfilling about stepping outside of yourself and showing up for someone else. A kind word. A helping hand. Listening when it would be easier to scroll. It doesn’t have to be grand. Most of the time, it’s the small, unnoticed moments that matter most. Funny thing is, when you start pouring into others, you realize your own cup doesn’t run dry like you thought it would.

Y is for Yourself.
Now this part tends to get misunderstood. Putting yourself last doesn’t mean ignoring yourself. It doesn’t mean running on empty or pretending you’re fine when you’re not. It means trusting that when your priorities are in the right order, you’ll still be taken care of. It means giving yourself grace, taking time to breathe, and recognizing that you’re human. You matter too. But you don’t have to be at the center of everything to find peace.

And that’s really what this all comes down to. Order.

We spend so much of our lives trying to rearrange things. Putting ourselves first, chasing happiness, trying to control outcomes. And somehow, we end up feeling more off-balance than ever. But when you flip it, when you go back to something simple like J-O-Y, things start to settle.

Jesus. Others. Yourself.

Not because it’s easy. Not because it’s trendy. But because it works.

Joy isn’t loud. It doesn’t demand attention. It shows up in quiet moments. In conversations that matter. In showing up for people. In trusting God when you don’t have all the answers.

And maybe that’s the lesson here. Joy was never meant to be complicated. We just forgot how to keep it simple.

Remember, be kind to yourself and to others.
